FASTRESS 02 Analysis

1 Preparation

1.1 Data import

1.2 Import checks

2 Analysis sample setup

  • dat_main = fixed main sample from the cleaning script

  • ids_complete = exactly those individuals from this main sample

  • dat_main_final = the same dataset plus order_group

2.1 Main sample

Create fixed analysis samples

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  n_rows n_sessions n_conditions     n
   <int>      <int>        <int> <int>
1      2          2            2    20

2.2 Sample overview

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_total_sessions n_total_persons
             <int>           <int>
1               60              40
# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_sessions n_persons
       <int>     <int>
1         40        20
# A tibble: 1 × 1
  n_complete_persons
               <int>
1                 20
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  planned_condition n_sessions n_persons
  <fct>                  <int>     <int>
1 satiated                  20        20
2 fasted                    20        20

3 Order and carryover

3.1 Inspect session order structure

Check whether the order of the conditions is relevant:

  • whether participants were fasting or satiated first

  • whether order has a main effect

  • whether order moderates the effect of planned_condition

3.2 Crossover check

Verify crossover structure

# A tibble: 1 × 2
      n    nn
  <int> <int>
1     2    20
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  session planned_condition     n
  <fct>   <fct>             <int>
1 T1      satiated             10
2 T1      fasted               10
3 T2      satiated             10
4 T2      fasted               10

3.3 Order variable

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  order_group        n
  <fct>          <int>
1 satiated_first    10
2 fasted_first      10

3.4 Merge order

Merge order variable into the main sample

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  n_sessions n_conditions     n
       <int>        <int> <int>
1          2            2    20
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  n_rows n_ids n_missing_order
   <int> <int>           <int>
1     40    20               0
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  order_group    session planned_condition     n
  <fct>          <fct>   <fct>             <int>
1 satiated_first T1      satiated             10
2 satiated_first T2      fasted               10
3 fasted_first   T1      fasted               10
4 fasted_first   T2      satiated             10

3.5 Order descriptives

# A tibble: 4 × 6
  order_group    planned_condition n_obs n_ids total_kcal_M total_kcal_SD
  <fct>          <fct>             <int> <int>        <dbl>         <dbl>
1 satiated_first satiated             10    10         307.          148.
2 satiated_first fasted               10    10         347.          192.
3 fasted_first   satiated             10    10         438.          141.
4 fasted_first   fasted               10    10         402.          172.

3.6 Order models

Test three models:
A) a baseline model without order;
B) plus the main effect of order;
C) plus the interaction between planned_condition and order_group.

-> If C does not perform better than B, there is no evidence that the condition effect depends on the order.

Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_base: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + (1 | subject)
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_base         4 512.81 519.57 -252.41    504.81                     
m_order_add    5 512.88 521.32 -251.44    502.88 1.9329  1     0.1644
Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
m_order_int: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_order_add    5 512.88 521.32 -251.44    502.88                     
m_order_int    6 512.59 522.72 -250.29    500.59 2.2907  1     0.1302
Linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood . t-tests use Satterthwaite's
  method [lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)
   Data: dat_main_order

      AIC       BIC    logLik -2*log(L)  df.resid 
    512.6     522.7    -250.3     500.6        34

Interpretation

If anova(m_order_add, m_order_int) is not significant –> then there is no evidence that order moderates the condition effect

If anova(m_base, m_order_add) is significant –> then there is a general difference between fasted_first and satiated_first, independent of condition

9 Model specification

9.1 Random effects

First, use a random intercept as the main model

Then test the random slope

  • Only include the random slope if the model is stable

  • no major warnings appear

  • and the fit is visibly better

9.2 Model helpers

Define reporting functions for mixed models

10 Main analyses

10.1 Condition effects

Data: dat_main_final
Models:
m0_total: total_kcal ~ order_group + (1 | subject)
m1_total: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)

10.2 Session Effects

Data: dat_main_final
Models:
m_period0: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
m_period1: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + session + order_group + (1 | subject)
          npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)

11 Moderation analyses

11.1 Moderation helper functions

11.1.1 Moderation 1: SRS

m_srs_add = main effect of Condition plus main effect of Moderator

m_srs_int = plus interaction; ANOVA tests whether the interaction provides additional explanatory power

# A tibble: 1 × 6
  n_rows n_ids n_nonmiss_outcome n_nonmiss_cond n_nonmiss_mod
   <int> <int>             <int>          <int>         <int>
1     40    20                40             40            40

12 Sensitivity analyses

12.1 Mismatch check

What: Test robustness by excluding sessions with mismatches.

Why: This demonstrates that the main results are not driven by non-compliance. If I consider only the metabolically consistent sessions, does the main effect still hold?

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  n_rows n_ids n_missing_order
   <int> <int>           <int>
1     40    20               0
# A tibble: 1 × 4
  n_rows n_sessions n_conditions     n
   <int>      <int>        <int> <int>
1      2          2            2    19

Mit allen Sessions:
                        Estimate Std. Error       df   t value        Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             325.9398   48.06509 23.08195 6.7812152 0.0000006340661
planned_conditionfasted   2.0285   25.78292 20.00000 0.0786761 0.9380721112771
order_groupfasted_first  93.2855   65.48379 20.00000 1.4245586 0.1697026138224

Ohne mismatch Sessions:
                          Estimate Std. Error       df   t value      Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             315.815702   51.43830 21.81385 6.1396992 0.00000365489
planned_conditionfasted   3.755263   27.08204 19.00000 0.1386625 0.89117596795
order_groupfasted_first 102.546167   68.40194 19.00000 1.4991704 0.15026197953

12.2 Log transform

Log transformation typically:

  • makes large values less dominant

  • makes the distribution more symmetrical

  • often produces slightly cleaner residuals

What: Analyze log1p(total_kcal) as a robustness check. Does my main effect persist if I statistically reduce the skewness of the outcome distribution?

Data: dat
Models:
m0: f_m0
m1: f_m1
   npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
